skill.name: router
skill.description: Routes Claude Code requests to suitable skills, agents, and slash commands using intent, context, and workflow patterns.
skill.summary: Selects and coordinates the right Claude Code tool for development tasks.
skill.author: AdamManuel-dev

security_audit.summary: Static findings were largely false positives caused by markdown code fences and substring matches such as "description" and "design". No prompt injection, malware, credential access, or network exfiltration evidence was found. The remaining concern is medium risk because the skill explicitly coordinates slash commands, agents, git operations, and an emergency workflow that skips confirmations.

medium_findings.title: Automated Command Orchestration With Reduced Confirmation
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The skill is designed to invoke skills, agents, and slash commands, including git-related commands. The production emergency workflow sets skip_confirmations to true and tells the assistant to execute without confirmation, which can increase the chance of unintended changes or commits if the router misclassifies urgency.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections explicitly describe tool invocation and an emergency workflow that skips confirmations. The behavior is legitimate for a routing skill, but it can trigger external commands and git actions if used without human oversight.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fence Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The external command detections come from markdown examples and fenced blocks that document routing patterns. They are not executable Ruby backtick calls or shell code in this repository.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced content is documentation in markdown fences and routing examples. No executable Ruby or script file exists in the scanned file set.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The high-severity weak cryptography alerts match ordinary words such as "description" and "design" or routing labels. No cryptographic API, hash algorithm implementation, or password handling evidence was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Context review shows these matches are frontmatter descriptions and routing terminology. There is no semantic evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or other weak cryptographic usage.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Benign Routing References
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: System and network reconnaissance alerts map to routing workflow terms, available reviewer commands, or error recovery text. No evidence found of network scanning, host enumeration, or data collection commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations describe command routing and reviewer names, not reconnaissance behavior. No scanner, socket, or network request implementation appears in the files.

dangerous_patterns.title: No-Confirmation Emergency Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The emergency workflow explicitly skips confirmations and includes fixes, security review, and git commit steps. This is not malicious, but marketplace users should understand that urgent keywords can route to automated command execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow contains explicit skip_confirmations behavior and user-facing text that says no confirmations are required. The risk is operational rather than malicious.

content.value_statement: Choosing the right Claude Code tool can slow down complex development work. This skill analyzes intent and context, then routes tasks to suitable skills, agents, commands, or workflows.
actual_capabilities: Classifies user requests by action, domain, scope, urgency, and multi-step intent.
Maps common development tasks to Claude Code skills, agents, and slash commands.
Uses project context such as git state, diagnostics, and file types to improve routing.
Defines sequential, parallel, and hybrid workflow templates for development tasks.
Provides communication templates for high, medium, and low confidence routing decisions.
Documents fallback strategies for ambiguous requests, unavailable tools, and dependency conflicts.
limitations: It is documentation and routing guidance, not a standalone executable router.
It assumes the referenced Claude Code skills, agents, and slash commands are available.
Routing quality depends on accurate project context and clear user intent.
Emergency workflows can be too aggressive if urgent keywords are used casually.

use_cases.title: Choose the Right Tool Quickly
use_cases.description: Route a vague coding request to the best Claude Code command, skill, or agent.
use_cases.target_user: Developers new to Claude Code workflows

use_cases.title: Coordinate Multi-Step Development
use_cases.description: Plan feature work, implementation, fixes, review, and commit steps in a structured order.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Automation
use_cases.description: Use shared routing patterns so team members handle fixes, reviews, docs, and tests consistently.

prompt_templates.title: Route a Simple Task
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request with one clear goal.
prompt_templates.prompt: Route this request to the best Claude Code tool: fix my TypeScript errors.

prompt_templates.title: Pick Between Similar Tools
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request where several tools may fit.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this request and explain the best route plus one alternative: review my authentication changes.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Workflow Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Multi-step request that needs ordering and dependencies.
prompt_templates.prompt: Route this multi-step task into an efficient workflow: plan, implement, test, and commit a dashboard feature.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Routing Decision
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request that needs risk and confidence analysis.
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this routing decision for safety, confidence, and missing context before execution: emergency fix and commit production issues now.

output_examples.input: Fix all failing tests and then commit the changes.
output_examples.output: The router identifies a sequential workflow: run the test-fix command first, verify results, then use the git commit command if checks pass.

output_examples.input: Build a new settings page with backend validation.
output_examples.output: The router recommends a feature workflow with planning first, parallel frontend and TypeScript implementation, quality checks, and review.
The router lowers confidence, gathers context, and asks a clarifying question if diagnostics do not reveal an obvious next step.

best_practices: Use the router when a request could map to several Claude Code tools.
Review proposed routes before allowing workflows that commit, stash, or deploy changes.
Keep the available tool inventory current so routing recommendations stay accurate.
anti_patterns: Do not use emergency keywords unless the task truly needs fast-path execution.
Do not route destructive git or deployment actions without checking project state first.
Do not rely on the router when the user explicitly named the exact tool to use.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It analyzes a development request and recommends the most suitable Claude Code skill, agent, command, or workflow.

faq.question: Does it execute code by itself?
faq.answer: The files are markdown guidance. The skill describes how an assistant should invoke available Claude Code tools.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: It can route to external commands and includes an emergency workflow that skips confirmations. This requires user oversight.

faq.question: Can it help beginners?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains routing choices and teaches users which Claude Code tools fit common development tasks.

faq.question: What tools does it reference?
faq.answer: It references Claude Code skills, Task agents, and slash commands for fixes, reviews, planning, documentation, testing, and git work.

faq.question: When should I avoid it?
faq.answer: Avoid it when the desired tool is already clear, or when automatic command routing could affect sensitive changes.
